contents: 12 Review3 Injections of platelet-rich plasma for androgenic alopecia:4 A systematic review5 F. PicardQ1*, B. Hersant, J. Niddam, J.-P. Meningaud6 Department of Plastic & Reconstructive Surgery and Maxillo-Facial Surgery, Henri-Mondor Hospital, 51 avenue du Mare'chal-de-Lattre-de-Tassigny,7 Cre'teil 94010, France89 1. Introduction10 Androgenic alopecia, which is explained by abnormally11 elevated activity of the enzyme 5a-reductase type 2 at the level12 of the hair follicles in the temporal, frontal and vertex areas [1],13 induces an esthetic prejudice. Injections of platelet-rich plasma14 (PRP) into the scalp are one of the new therapies used to slow down15 the onset of androgenic alopecia and to treat it. PRP is an16 increasingly widely used biomaterial prepared by recovering,17 separating and concentrating patient platelets from a blood draw18 [2]. The preparation methods are increasingly well mastered and19 abundantly described in the literature, including methods20 employing commercial kits [3]. Platelets are cells of interest with21 respect to cell proliferation and differentiation since they can22 release numerous growth factors and cytokines [4], which attract23 and stimulate endothelial and stem cells [5].24 Hair growth, known as the anagen phase, consists of the25 proliferation and maturation of progenitor cells derived from the26 bulge stem cells [6,7]. The cells giving rise to hair growth are of at27 least 3 types [8]: slow-multiplying stem cells present in the hair

28follicle bulge, fast-multiplying progenitor cells which derive from29stem cells and mesenchymal cells which are present in the dermal30papilla.31Certain growth factors, of which EGF, FGF and TGF, contribute to32hair growth regulation and control as shown in several studies33[7,9-11]. Those growth factors are present in PRP [4] and thus34available, from a blood sample, in an autologous manner, i.e. they35are derived from the patient himself.36PRP injections may stimulate the growth of hair affected by37locally increased activity of the enzyme 5a-reductase. Currently,38the reference treatment is hair transplantation, which is an39onerous surgical procedure requiring a donor site. This makes PRP40injections attractive if they are effective.41The aim of this literature review was to evaluate the efficacy of42PRP injections into the scalp of patients with androgenic alopecia,43to determine the methods of use and to identify any adverse44effects.452. Material and methods46A literature review was conducted in February 2017 after47searching the Pubmed and the Google Scholar databases with theJ Stomatol Oral Maxillofac Surg xxx (2017) xxx-xxx* Corresponding author.E-mail address: (F. Picard).A R T I C L E I N F OArticle history:Received 2 April 2017Accepted 20 June 2017Keywords:Platelet-rich plasmaAlopeciaAndrogenic alopeciaReview

A B S T R A C TThe aim of this literature review was to evaluate the effects of PRP injections into the scalp of patientswith androgenic alopecia. A literature review was conducted using the Pubmed and Google Scholardatabases with the search terms ''platelet-rich plasma'' or ''platelet-rich fibrin'' and ''hair'' or ''alopecia''or ''androgenic alopecia''. The publications included had to clinically assess the efficacy of PRP injectionsin patients with androgenic alopecia. Out of the 32 publications retrieved, 14 publications were included,of which 3 randomized, 4 prospective controlled, 4 prospective uncontrolled and 3 retrospective studies.Seven out of 9 studies reported a significant increase of hair density ranging between 12.3 and 45.9 hairs/cm2, (i.e. 19-31% hairs/cm2). Four studies assessed hair loss with the traction test and found a negativeresult after treatment in more than 95% of patients. Regarding hair thickness, 1 study reported anincrease in hair diameter of 46.4% and 1 reported an increase of 106.4% of the ''Hair mass index''. Overall,the use of PRP injections in patients with androgenic alopecia seems effective with respect to promotinglost hair regrowth, decreasing hair loss and increasing hair thickness. The effects appear to beprogressive from the first injection session, to peak after 3 to 5 sessions and to be attenuated in theabsence of further injections. No major adverse effect was reported in the 14 clinical studies.C 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.Available online at

ScienceDirectwww.sciencedirect.com 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.48 search terms ''platelet-rich plasma'' or ''platelet-rich fibrin'' and49 ''hair'' or ''alopecia'' or ''androgenic alopecia''.50 In order to be included, the publication had to clinically assess51 the efficacy of PRP injections in patients with androgenic alopecia.52 There was no publication date deadline. The publications could be53 written in English, French, Spanish, Italian or German.54 The exclusion criteria consisted of the following: case reports,55 letters or reviews without any clinical trial, studies assessing PRP56 injections in association with another procedure or treatment,57 studies in patients with alopecia of a non-androgenic etiology, and58 in vitro and animal in vivo studies.59 The primary endpoint was the hair regrowth as assessed by hair60 density, i.e. the number of hairs/unit area. The validated methods61 of measurement of hair density are phototrichograms [12],62 phototrichoscans [13] (which is a modified computerized Photo-63 trichogram), and validated scores like the ''Hair density Index''64 [14], which is the product of hair density and hair thickness65 assessed at the same time.66 The secondary endpoint was hair loss, which can be assessed by67 the traction test [12]. This validated and well described method is68 the number of extracted hairs when a clinician grasps between his

69thumb, index and middle finger a bundle of approximately 50-6070hairs and tug them away firmly.71The others endpoints were hair thickness, which can be assessed72by the hair diameter, the injections protocol and the side effects.733. Results74The search retrieved 32 publications of which 18 were excluded75for the following reasons: 5 publications consisted of letters76without any clinical trial [15-19]; 2 publication were literature77reviews which did not focus on the use of PRP for androgenic78alopecia [20,21]; 1 publication was only an abstract [22];791 publication was a case report [23]; 2 publications evaluated80PRP injections as adjunctive therapy with polydeoxyribonucleo-81tide injections [24], or with deltaparine and protamine micro82particles [25]; 1 publication evaluated hair transplants soaked83with PRP before transplantation [26]; 3 publications evaluated PRP84injections in patients with alopecia areata [27-29] and 3 publica-85tions were in vitro or animal in vivo studies [30-32].86The flowchart is presented in Fig. 1. JStomatol Oral Maxillofac Surg (2017), 88 Overall, 14 clinical trials assessed PRP injections alone in89 patients with androgenic alopecia and were included in the review,90 of which 3 randomized study [33-35] (level of evidence 2 [36]),91 4 prospective controlled studies [37-40] (level of evidence 2 [36]),92 4 prospective uncontrolled studies [41-44] (level of evidence 493 [36]), and 3 retrospective studies [45-47] (level of evidence 4 [36]).

94 3.1. Hair density95 Among the 14 studies included, 9 studies assessed hair density96 and 7 found an increase of hair density: 2 randomized controlled97 studies [33,34], 2 prospective controlled studies [39,40] and98 3 prospective uncontrolled studies [42-44].99 The study of Gentile et al. [33] was a randomized double-100 blinded trial on 23 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of101 PRP on a monthly basis or 3 sessions of injections of physiological102 solutions. Hair density was assessed with Trichoscans and the103 results showed a mean increase of 45.9 hair/cm2 in the treated104 group versus a mean decrease of 5.6 hair/cm2 in the control group105 (P < 0.05).106 The study of Alves et al. [34] was a randomized double-blinded107 trial on 25 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of PRP on a108 monthly basis or 3 sessions of injections of physiological solutions.109 Hair density was assessed with Trichoscans and the results showed110 a mean increase of 12.8 hair/cm2 in the treated group versus a111 mean decrease of 2.1 hair/cm2 in the control group (P < 0.05).

112The study of Cervelli et al. [39] was a prospective controlled113study on 10 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of PRP in114one half of the alopecic scalp and 3 sessions of physiological115solutions in the other half. Hair density was assessed with116Trichoscans and the results showed a mean increase of 27.1 hair/117cm2 in the treated areas and a mean decrease of 2.1 hair/cm2 in the118control areas (P < 0.05).119The study of Kang et al. [40] was a prospective controlled study120on 26 patients treated with 2 sessions of injections of PRP at an121interval of 3 months or with weekly injections of placental extract.122Hair density was assessed with phototrichograms and the results123showed a mean increase of 29.2% of the hair density in the PRP124group (P < 0.0001).125The study of Gkini et al. [42] was a prospective uncontrolled126study on 20 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of PRP at127an interval of 3 weeks. Hair density was assessed with photo-128trichograms and the results showed a mean increase of the hair129density from 143.1 hair/cm2 to 170.7 hair/cm2 (P < 0.05).130The study of Khatu et al. [43] was a prospective uncontrolled131study on 11 patients treated with 4 sessions of injections of PRP132every 2 weeks. Hair density was assessed with phototrichograms133and the results showed a mean increase of the hair density from13471 to 93.1 hair/cm2 (no statistical analysis).135The study of Sclafani [44] was a prospective uncontrolled study136on 15 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of PRP on a137monthly basis. 138 [14], taking into account both the number of hairs and the139 thickness of hairs, assessed with a specific device (Hair Check, Divi140 international Co. C ). The results showed a gradual increase in the141 hair density index up to a mean increase of 106.4% (P < 0.05)142 1 month after the third injections session. The index decreased to143 75.1% 4 months after the last injection session.144 Two studies did not find an increase of hair density. The study of145 Mapar et al. [35] was a randomized single-blinded trial on146 17 patients treated with 2 sessions of injections of PRP on a147 monthly basis or 2 sessions of injections of physiological solutions.148 The results showed a mean decrease of 2.2 hair/cm2 in the treated149 group and a mean decrease of 0.2 hair/cm2 in the control group150 (P > 0.05). The study of Puig et al. [37] was a prospective double-151 blinded controlled study on 26 patients treated with 1 session of152 injections of PRP or 1 session of injections of physiological solutions.153 Hair density was assessed with phototrichograms and the results154 showed no improvement in hair count or hair mass index.155 3.2. Hair thickness

156 Hair thickness has been objectively measured in 2 studies. In157 the study of Kang et al. [40], hair diameter increased on average of158 31.3% (P < 0.0001) 3 months after the first injection session and159 46.4% (P < 0.0001) 3 months after the second injection session.160 In the study of Sclafani [44], the ''Hair density index'' [14] (was161 composed of the hair density and the hair thickness). A part of the162 increase of this index up to 106.4% was due to the increase of the163 hair diameter, as it was shown on pre- and post-treatment164 pictures.165 Hair volume has been subjectively assessed in 4 studies. In the166 study of Betsi et al. [46], which was a retrospective uncontrolled167 study on 42 patients treated with 5 sessions of PRP at an interval of168 2 weeks, in the 2 studies of Navarro et al. [45,47] which were169 retrospective studies on patients treated with 2 sessions of170 injections of PRP at an interval of 1 month and in the study of171 Puig et al. [37], in which 33.3% of patients in the treated group172 versus 27.2% in the minoxidil group (P > 0.05) found an improve-173 ment of their hair thickness.174 3.3. Hair loss

175 Hair loss has been assessed in 4 studies by the traction test176 which became negative in over 95% of patients.177 The study of Singhal et al. [38] was a prospective controlled178 study on 20 patients treated with 4 sessions of injections of PRP at179 an interval of 2 weeks. The traction test result was negative for all180 patients after having received 4 PRP injections sessions.181 The study of Gkini et al. [42] was a prospective uncontrolled182 study on 20 patients treated with 3 sessions of injections of PRP at183 an interval of 3 weeks and a last session 6 months after. The184 traction test result was negative in all patients after the first of185 3 injections sessions and became positive again 3 months after the186 last PRP injection session. The effect appeared to be transient.187 In the study of Kathu et al. [43], the traction test result was188 negative in 9 out of 11 patients (82%) after 4 sessions of PRP189 injections at an interval of 2 weeks.190 The study of Betsi et al. [46] was a retrospective uncontrolled191 study on 42 patients treated with 5 sessions of PRP at an interval of192 2 weeks. The traction test result was negative for all the patients193 after the third injections session.194 In the study of Puig et al. [37] (1 session of PRP injection), 26.6%195 of patients in the treated group vs. 27.3% of patients in the control196 group (P > 0.05) found an improvement of hair loss. The high score197 of subjective improvement of hair loss in the control group can198 only be due to the placebo effect since physiological solution was199 injected instead of PRP.2003.4. Patient satisfaction

201In the prospective uncontrolled studies of Gkini et al. [42] and202Khatu et al. [43], the mean patient satisfaction rate were203respectively 7.1 and 7.0/10. In the retrospective uncontrolled204study of Navarro et al. [47] on 100 patients treated with 2 sessions205of PRP injections, ''all the patients were satisfied'' at the end of the206follow-up, i.e. 3 months after the last injection.2073.5. Side effects208No major adverse effect was reported in the 14 studies. No209iatrogenic alopecia, infection or abscess was reported. PRP210injections seemed to be very safe.211Five studies [38,40,42,43,46] reported a few episodes of local212pain, erythema and swelling post-injections but there was no213infection. In the study of Singhal et al. [38], some patients214experienced headache post-injections, which regressed on para-215cetamol.2163.6. Histological results217The histological analysis of the PRP-treated areas in the studies218of Cervelli et al. [39] and Gentile et al. [33] showed a significant219increase in the number of hair follicles, an increase in the thickness220of the scalp epidermis, an increase in vascularization between the221follicles and an increase in the Ki67 proliferation index [48] of222bulge stem cells and of the cells of the basal layer of the epidermis.223In the retrospectives studies of Navarro et al. [45,47], the224proportion of the anagen follicles increases of 6.9% in the PRP group225versus 4.6% in the minoxidil group (P < 0.05%) whereas the226proportion of telogen follicles decreased of 5.7% in the PRP group227versus 2.6% in the minoxidil group (P < 0.05).2283.7. Methods of injections

229The total amount of PRP injected per treatment was between2301.5 mL and 12 mL: 6 studies [34,35,40,43,45,47] injected less than2314 mL and 7 studies [33,37-39,41,44,46] injected 8 to 12 mL. The232volume per injection was described in 7 studies [33,34,39-42,44],233and was between 0.05 and 0.15 mL/cm2in 6 studies234[33,34,39,40,42,44]. The number of injections per session of235injections was described in 7 studies [33,34,39-42,44], about23620 injections were made in the study of Alves et al. [34], about23750 injections were made in 3 studies [40-42] and about238100 injections in 3 other studies [33,39,44]. The depth of injections239was described in 5 studies [35,37,41,42,44], 3 studies [35,42,44]240did intradermal injections, 1 study [37] did subcutaneous241injections and 1 study [41] injected in the ''superficial scalp''.242The localisation of the injections was described in 8 studies243[33,34,39,40,42-45] and was always the zones of androgenic244alopecia, i.e. the frontal, parietal and vertex areas. Regarding the245number of sessions of injections, 1 study [37] assessed 1 session,2465 studies [35,40,41,45,47] assessed 2 sessions, 5 studies247[33,34,39,42,44] assessed 3 sessions, 2 studies [38,43] assessed2484 sessions and 1 study [46] assessed 5 sessions. The intervals249between each session of treatment were 2 weeks in2503 studies [38,43,46], 3 weeks in 1 study [42], 1 month in 7 studies251[33-35,39,44,45,47] and 3 months in 2 studies [40,41].2523.8. Methods of preparation of PRP253In 12 studies of this review [33-35,37,39-42,44-47], the PRP254was made with commercial kits like SelphylC[33,39,44],255RegenlabC[42,46], ProtealC[34], TubexC[35], AngelC[37],256SmartprepC [40], BiometC [41], and BTIC [45,47]. The PRP was

G ModelJORMAS 65 1-7Please cite this article in press as: Picard F, et al. Injections of platelet-rich plasma for androgenic alopecia: A systematic review. JStomatol Oral Maxillofac Surg (2017), 257 mixed with calcium before the injections in order to be activated in258 11 studies [33-35,38,39,42-47]. Five studies [35,37,40-42]259 assessed the platelet concentration of the PRP used and found260 that it was increased between 2.75 [37] and 5.9 [40] times261 compared to the baseline.262 4. Discussion

263 Fourteen clinical studies specifically assessing PRP injections in264 patients with androgenic alopecia were included in this literature265 review. However, these studies did not have the same protocol for266 the frequency and the amount of injections. Regarding the267 methods of evaluation, they used the most common objective268 non-invasive methods, which are phototrichograms [12], photo-269 trichoscans [13], the ''Hair density index'' [14] and the measure of270 the hair diameter. To be accurate, these methods need to assess the271 exact same location on the scalp, which is not easily feasible in272 practice. Others studies used global pictures [12] and traction tests273 [12], which are much more subjective, but these methods are274 reproducible and do not need to assess the exact same location of275 the scalp.276 Regarding hair regrowth, 7 studies (2 randomized trials [33,34],277 2 prospective controlled [39,40] and 3 prospective uncontrolled278 studies [42-44]) showed a significant increase in the number of279 hairs ranging between 12.3 and 45.9 hairs/cm2 (i.e. 19% to 31%),280 whereas 2 studies did not (1 randomized trial [35] and281 1 prospective controlled study [37]).282 Regarding hair loss, all the results were concordant and283 clinically interesting. The traction test was assessed in 4 studies284 and was negative for more than 95% [38,42,43,46] of the patients285 treated with PRP injections. Furthermore, histological studies by286 Navarro et al. [45,47] found an increase of the anagen follicles287 proportion and a decrease of the telogen follicles proportion after288 2 PRP injections, which can explain the improvement of the289 traction test and hair loss.290 Regarding hair thickness and hair volume, 2 studies found very291 promising results since one showed an increase of the hair292 diameter of almost 50% [40] and the other found an increase of the293 ''Hair mass index'' of more than 100% [44].294 Furthermore, 8 studies [37,38,41,42,44-47] found subjective295 improvements whether it was for hair loss, hair volume, hair296 quality or scalp coverage.297 With regard to histology, 2 studies [33,39] showed similar298 results that may underlie the clinical efficacy of PRP injections: an299 increase in the Ki67 index of cells at bulge level, and an increase in300 local vascularization which may improve hair growth.301 In vitro and animal in vivo studies have also shown the effects of302 PRP at the cell level. The in vivo studies by Li et al. [30] and303 Rastegard et al. [32] generated similar results showing that PRP304 increased the proliferation of the cells of the dermal papilla and the305 activity of the signaling pathways involved in hair growth.306 Moreover, the study of Miao et al. [31] evidenced that the307 adjunction of PRP with epidermal stem cells and cells of the dermal308 papilla increased the formation of new follicles and the growth rate309 of murine fur.310 Therefore, despite the lack of high level of evidence data, PRP311 injections into the scalp of patients with androgenic alopecia312 seemed to have 3 different ways of efficiency: hair regrowth,313 decrease of hair loss and increase of hair thickness.314 Only minor side effects, like local pain, erythema and swelling315 were reported in the 14 studies included. There was no infection,316 no abscess and no iatrogenic alopecia reported. Thus, injections of317 PRP for androgenic alopecia seemed to be a very safe procedure.318 The analysis of the injections methods showed that most of the319 studies [33,34,39,40,42,44] realized injections of about 0.1 mL/

320cm2, and injected the PRP in or just below the dermis321[35,37,41,42,44].322The analysis of the protocols did not enable the determination323of a number of sessions or a between-session interval that was324more effective since the studies were not sufficiently numerous,325the number of injection sessions ranged from 1 to 5, and the326between-session intervals ranged from 2 weeks to 3 months.327However, 2 studies suggested that the effects were transient. The328study of Sclafani et al. [44] showed that the ''Hair density index''329decreased 4 months after the last session and the study of Gkini330et al. [42] generated similar results with an increase of the traction331test and a decrease of the hair density 4 months after the last332injections session.333The PRP used were prepared with commercial kits in 12 studies334[33-35,37,39-42,44-47] and were activated with calcium in33511 studies [33-35,38,39,42-47]. Even though the control of the336platelet concentration seems to be crucial, only 5 studies337[35,37,40-42] controlled their PRP, which did not enable the338determination of a more effective platelet concentration for339androgenic alopecia.340Maintenance treatment seems therefore indispensable in order341to delay the long-term progression of androgenic alopecia. An342appropriate protocol for use of PRP may be an initial treatment343with 3 injections sessions at an interval of 1 month and a344maintenance treatment every 3 months.3455. Conclusion346Overall, the use of PRP injections in patients with androgenic347alopecia seems effective in promoting lost hair regrowth, in348decreasing hair loss and in increasing hair thickness. The effects349appeared to be gradual, to peak after on average 4 sessions, and to350decrease when the injections stopped, hence the need of a351maintenance treatment. No major side effects were reported.352Disclosure of interest353The authors declare that they have no competing interest.354References
